title: The Ward: Toronto's First Immigrant Neighbourhood
description: A dense downtown neighbourhood where successive waves of newcomers settled from the 1840s to the 1950s. Irish, Jewish, Black, Italian, and Chinese communities all called it home before it was demolished for hospitals, New City Hall, and Nathan Phillips Square.
transcript: In the heart of what is now Nathan Phillips Square stood one of Toronto's most storied neighbourhoods. The Ward was home to successive waves of immigrants from the 1840s onward.

entities.name: The Ward
entities.description: Historic immigrant neighbourhood bounded by College, Queen, Yonge, and University. Demolished in the 1950s-60s.

entities.name: Nathan Phillips Square
entities.description: Toronto's main civic square, built 1965 on the former site of The Ward.

entities.name: Urban Renewal
entities.description: Post-war policy of demolishing so-called slum neighbourhoods for modernist redevelopment.

title: Little Portugal: Dundas West's Cultural Heart
description: A vibrant neighbourhood settled by Portuguese immigrants in the 1950s-60s. Centred along Dundas Street West, known for bakeries, churrasqueiras, and the annual Portugal Day celebrations.
transcript: Along Dundas Street West between Ossington and Dufferin, a community built itself brick by brick. Portuguese families arrived in Toronto starting in the 1950s and 60s.

entities.name: Little Portugal
entities.description: Toronto neighbourhood along Dundas West, settled primarily by Portuguese immigrants beginning in the 1950s.

entities.name: Portugal Day
entities.description: Annual celebration of Portuguese culture held in June along Dundas Street West.

entities.name: Dundas Street West
entities.description: Major east-west thoroughfare through Little Portugal, lined with bakeries and restaurants.

title: The Buried Bridge: Taddle Creek's Hidden World
description: A 6km stream once flowed through the University of Toronto campus to the harbour. Buried underground in the 1860s-1880s, its ghost still shapes the city above.
transcript: Beneath your feet, a river still flows. Taddle Creek once wound through what is now the University of Toronto, crossed by bridges at Yonge Street before emptying into the harbour.

entities.name: Taddle Creek
entities.description: A buried 6km stream running from near St. Clair through U of T to the harbour, converted to sewers in the 1860s-1880s.

entities.name: Philosopher's Walk
entities.description: A pathway at the University of Toronto that follows the old Taddle Creek ravine.

entities.name: Lost Rivers
entities.description: Toronto has dozens of buried streams. Taddle Creek is the most famous, but Garrison Creek, Castle Frank Brook, and others also flow underground.

title: Yorkville: From Hippie Haven to High Fashion
description: Now Toronto's luxury shopping district, Yorkville was the epicentre of Canada's 1960s counterculture. Coffeehouses hosted Joni Mitchell, Neil Young, and Gordon Lightfoot before gentrification transformed the neighbourhood.
transcript: In the 1960s, Yorkville was Toronto's answer to Greenwich Village. Coffeehouses like the Riverboat and the Purple Onion were launching pads for a generation of Canadian musicians.

entities.name: Yorkville
entities.description: Toronto neighbourhood that transformed from 1960s bohemian hub to luxury retail district.

entities.name: Joni Mitchell
entities.description: Canadian singer-songwriter who performed at Yorkville coffeehouses in the mid-1960s before moving to the US.

entities.name: The Riverboat Coffee House
entities.description: Legendary Yorkville folk music venue at 134 Yorkville Avenue, operated 1964-1978.

title: The Candy Bar Strike of 1947
description: When chocolate bar prices rose from 5 to 8 cents, 500 Toronto high school students marched down Bloor Street to Christie Pits in a nationwide youth boycott that collapsed when organizers were red-baited during the Cold War.
transcript: In 1947, the price of a chocolate bar went up from five cents to eight cents. To Canadian kids, this was an outrage worth fighting.

entities.name: Christie Pits Park
entities.description: West Toronto park that was the rally point for the 1947 candy bar strike. Also the site of the 1933 Christie Pits riot.

entities.name: Candy Bar Boycott
entities.description: 1947 nationwide children's boycott against a 60% price increase on chocolate bars.

entities.name: Cold War Red Scare
entities.description: The Toronto Evening Telegram accused strike organizers of communist ties, causing the boycott to collapse.

title: Davenport Road: The 10,000-Year-Old Trail
description: One of the oldest roads in Toronto follows an Indigenous portage trail connecting the Don and Humber rivers along the ancient shoreline of glacial Lake Iroquois. It has been in continuous use for over 10,000 years.
transcript: Every time you drive along Davenport Road, you're following a path that has been walked for over ten thousand years. Long before Toronto existed, this was a portage trail.

entities.name: Davenport Road
entities.description: Road following an Indigenous portage trail called Gete-Onigaming, used for over 10,000 years.

entities.name: Lake Iroquois Shoreline
entities.description: Ancient glacial lake whose shoreline is now marked by the escarpment along Davenport Road.

entities.name: Ishpadinaa
entities.description: Anishinaabemowin name for the area, meaning 'high place.' Now marked on street signs along Davenport.

title: The Yonge Street Mall: Toronto's Sin Strip
description: In the 1970s, the stretch of Yonge Street between Dundas and Gerrard was lined with grindhouse theatres, body-rub parlours, and adult entertainment. The era ended after a tragic event in 1977 triggered a public cleanup campaign.
transcript: In the 1970s, if you walked along Yonge Street between Dundas and Gerrard, you entered a different world. This was Toronto's Sin Strip.

entities.name: Yonge Street Strip
entities.description: Four blocks of Yonge Street between Dundas and Gerrard, nicknamed 'The Sin Strip' in the 1970s.

entities.name: Yonge Street Cleanup
entities.description: Public campaign in the late 1970s that led to the removal of adult businesses from Yonge Street.

entities.name: Grindhouse Theatres
entities.description: Low-budget movie theatres showing exploitation films, a fixture of 1970s Yonge Street.

title: The Great Stork Derby
description: When Toronto lawyer Charles Vance Millar died in 1926, his will left his fortune to the Toronto woman who gave birth to the most children in the following 10 years. Four women each won for having nine children apiece.
transcript: On Halloween 1926, a Toronto lawyer named Charles Vance Millar died and left behind the most mischievous will in Canadian history.

entities.name: Charles Vance Millar
entities.description: Toronto lawyer whose 1926 will sparked the Great Stork Derby, leaving his fortune to whoever had the most babies in 10 years.

entities.name: The Great Stork Derby
entities.description: A 10-year contest (1926-1936) sparked by Millar's will. Four women tied with nine children each and split the fortune.

entities.name: Great Depression
entities.description: The contest ran through the Depression, making the prize money life-changing for working-class families.

title: Toronto's First NBA Game: The Huskies
description: The very first game in NBA history was played in Toronto on November 1, 1946, when the Toronto Huskies hosted the New York Knickerbockers at Maple Leaf Gardens. The Huskies folded after one season.
transcript: On November 1, 1946, the very first game in what would become the NBA was played right here in Toronto. The Toronto Huskies took on the New York Knickerbockers at Maple Leaf Gardens.

entities.name: Toronto Huskies
entities.description: Toronto's first professional basketball team, played one season (1946-47) in the Basketball Association of America.

entities.name: Maple Leaf Gardens
entities.description: Iconic arena at 60 Carlton Street, home to the Leafs from 1931-1999. Hosted the first NBA game in 1946.

entities.name: New York Knickerbockers
entities.description: The Knicks won the first NBA game 68-66 in Toronto on November 1, 1946.

title: The Jamaican Patty: Toronto's Signature Bite
description: The Jamaican beef patty became a Toronto icon through the Caribbean diaspora. In 1985, the government tried to ban the term 'beef patty,' sparking the Patty Wars. February 23 is now Patty Day in Toronto.
transcript: The Jamaican beef patty is as much a part of Toronto as the CN Tower. But getting it here took decades of work from the Caribbean community.

entities.name: Kensington Market
entities.description: Bohemian neighbourhood and open-air market, home to early patty shops like Patty King and Kensington Patty Palace.

entities.name: The Patty Wars
entities.description: 1985 battle when the Canadian government tried to ban the term 'beef patty' under the Meat Inspection Act. The Caribbean community fought back and won.

entities.name: Patty Day
entities.description: February 23 is officially Patty Day in Toronto, celebrating the Jamaican patty's cultural significance.
